topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The plastic stick sat on the kitchen counter like a damning piece of evidence. Liam stared at the results, his breath catching in his throat until it felt like sandpaper in his chest. The numbers swam before his eyes—percentages, match rates, familial links—all pointing toward a reality he hadn't dared to imagine. His hands trembled so violently he had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to steady himself. A low, involuntary sound escaped his lips, a choked gasp that sounded alien in the quiet morning. He slowly lowered the test, the paper crinkling under his touch, feeling a profound, sickening lurch in his stomach. Every familiar memory of his childhood felt suddenly overlaid with a thick, unwelcome fog of deception. He pushed himself back from the counter, his movements stiff and jerky, wanting desperately to incinerate the paper, but his fingers remained frozen above it.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." Sarah whispered, the words barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator. She reread the genetic report for the tenth time, her eyes tracing the lines of shared ancestry with a growing sense of nausea. The air in the small apartment seemed to thicken, pressing down on her lungs. She pressed the heels of her palms against her eyes, trying to physically block out the stark, undeniable truth laid bare on the screen. A cold sweat broke out along her hairline, plastering stray strands to her forehead. Her jaw clenched so tightly she could feel the muscles strain beneath her skin. When she finally looked up, the room seemed distorted, the familiar furniture suddenly alien and accusatory. She took a shaky step back, bumping against the wall, needing space, needing distance from the horrifying clarity of the data.

[story 3]

Mark sat bolt upright in his chair, the chair scraping loudly against the wooden floor. He hadn't processed the information; he was simply absorbing the sheer impossibility of it. The printed report lay spread across his lap, the stark genetic markers glowing under the harsh overhead light. He brought a hand up, not to touch the paper, but to press it flat against his forehead, as if trying to physically imprint the falsehood onto his skin. A deep, rattling sound emerged from his chest, a sound of utter collapse. His vision tunneled, the edges of the room blurring into an indistinct wash of color. He squeezed his eyes shut, fighting a rising wave of intense physical distress, his knuckles turning white as he gripped the edge of the desk until his fingers ached. He couldn't move; he was trapped by the sheer weight of what he had just discovered.
